name: Bitly
overview:
A URL shortener maps long URLs to short 6-8 character aliases and redirects users to the original URL when
the short link is accessed. The system must handle extremely high read traffic (redirects) with sub-10ms
latency, support custom aliases, track click analytics, and handle link expiration. Write traffic (link creation) is
orders of magnitude lower than read traffic. The system must be globally distributed to minimize redirect latency
for users worldwide.

inScope: Shorten a long URL to a unique short code (6-8 chars)
Redirect short URL to original URL with <10ms p99 latency
Track click analytics (count, geo, device, referrer)

items: Handle 100k URL creations/day and 10B redirects/day (~115k redirects/sec)
Redirect latency <10ms p99 — core user-facing SLA
99.99% availability for redirects; brief degradation for creation is tolerable
Read-heavy workload: optimize for redirect throughput over write throughput
Globally distributed: minimize redirect latency for users worldwide via CDN edge
Analytics pipeline can tolerate near-real-time delay (seconds to minutes)

entities.name: Short Link
entities.schema:
{
  short_code: string (6-8 chars, base62),
  long_url: string,
  user_id: string (nullable),
  created_at: timestamp,
  expires_at: timestamp (nullable),
  is_custom: boolean,

  click_count: int64
}

entities.name: Click Event
entities.schema:
{
  short_code: string,
  timestamp: ISO8601,
  ip: string,
  country: string,
  device_type: enum(MOBILE|DESKTOP|TABLET),
  referrer: string,
  user_agent: string
}

endpoints.response:
Location: <long_url>
  (301 = permanent, cached by browser; 302 = temporary, always hits server)

approaches.title: Auto-Increment ID + Base62 Encode
approaches.description: Use a database auto-increment ID and encode it in base62 (a-z, A-Z, 0-9). Simple but predictable: sequential IDs allow enumeration of all short links. Also creates a single-point bottleneck at the ID generator.
approaches.tradeoff: Enumerable IDs are a security risk; DB bottleneck at scale.

approaches.title: MD5/SHA256 Hash of Long URL
approaches.description: Hash the long URL and take the first 7 characters of the base62-encoded hash. Deterministic (same URL always gets same code) but collisions require retry logic. Two different users shortening the same URL get the same code — may be desired or not.
approaches.tradeoff: Hash collisions require retry; same URL always maps to same code (no per-user links).

approaches.title: Distributed ID Generator (Snowflake-style) + Base62
approaches.description: Use a Snowflake-style ID generator (timestamp + machine ID + sequence) to produce unique 64-bit IDs. Encode in base62 to get 7-8 character codes. IDs are unique, non-sequential (with bit shuffling), and generated without DB coordination. Multiple ID generator instances can run in parallel. Custom aliases bypass the generator and are stored directly after uniqueness check.
approaches.tradeoff: Requires running ID generator service; slight complexity over pure hash approach.

approaches.title: Redirect Strategy
approaches.description: 302 Temporary Redirect Always return 302 so every redirect hits the server, enabling accurate click counting. At 10B redirects/day (~115k/sec), even with caching this puts enormous load on origin servers.
approaches.tradeoff: High origin load; no browser-level caching benefit.

approaches.title: 301 Permanent Redirect with CDN
approaches.description: Return 301 so browsers cache the redirect. CDN edge nodes also cache the mapping. Dramatically reduces origin load but click counting becomes inaccurate (cached redirects never hit the server).
approaches.tradeoff: Click analytics are incomplete; cannot track repeat visits from same browser.

approaches.title: 302 + CDN Edge Caching + Async Analytics
approaches.description: Return 302 from CDN edge nodes. The CDN caches the short_code→long_url mapping with a short TTL (60s). Click events are logged at the CDN edge and streamed asynchronously to Kafka for analytics processing. This gives near-301 performance (CDN cache hit rate >99%) while maintaining accurate click counting via edge logs. Cache invalidation on link deletion is handled via CDN purge API.
approaches.tradeoff: CDN edge logging adds cost; slight analytics delay (near-real-time, not real-time).

components.name: API Service
components.responsibility: Handles URL creation, validation, custom alias checks; writes to primary

components.name: ID Generator
components.responsibility: Snowflake-style distributed ID service; produces unique 64-bit IDs

components.name: Redirect Service
components.responsibility: Stateless; looks up short_code in Redis cache, falls back to DB; returns 302

components.name: Redis Cache
components.responsibility: Stores short_code→long_url mappings; TTL matches link expiration; ~99% hit rate

components.name: PostgreSQL
components.responsibility: Primary store for short links; read replicas for redirect fallback

components.name: CDN (CloudFront)
components.responsibility: Caches redirects at edge; logs click events to S3

components.name: Kafka
components.responsibility: Streams click events from CDN logs to analytics pipeline

components.name: Analytics DB (Druid)
components.responsibility: Stores aggregated click analytics; serves dashboard queries

components.name: Cleanup Job
components.responsibility: Deletes expired links from DB and invalidates CDN cache

questions.title: 2. Cache Warming and Eviction
questions.content:
On startup, the redirect service pre-warms Redis with the top 1M most-clicked links (queried from the analytics
DB). New links are written to Redis immediately on creation. The cache uses an LRU eviction policy with a
24-hour TTL. For links with explicit expiration, the Redis TTL is set to match the link's expiry time, ensuring
automatic cleanup.

questions.title: 3. Analytics Pipeline
questions.content:
CDN access logs are streamed to S3 every minute. A Lambda function triggers on new S3 objects, parses the
logs, extracts click events, and publishes them to Kafka. A Flink job consumes Kafka, enriches events with
geo-IP lookup (MaxMind database), and writes aggregated counts to Druid. The Druid rollup pre-aggregates by
hour, reducing storage by 60x compared to raw event storage.

questions.title: 4. Handling 10B Redirects/Day
questions.content:
10B redirects/day = ~115k redirects/sec. With a CDN cache hit rate of 99%, only ~1,150 requests/sec reach the
origin redirect service. Redis handles these with sub-millisecond latency. The redirect service is stateless and
horizontally scalable behind a load balancer. Each Redis node handles ~100k ops/sec, so a 3-node Redis
cluster provides ample headroom.

questions.title: 5. Link Expiration
questions.content:
Expired links must return 404. Redis TTL handles cache expiration automatically. For DB cleanup, a
background job runs every hour and deletes links where expires_at < NOW(). The CDN cache is invalidated via
the CDN purge API when a link is deleted. To prevent serving stale cached redirects, the redirect service
checks the link's expiry time on cache miss and returns 410 Gone for expired links.

keyFlows: ① Create: Client → Create API → ID Generator → PostgreSQL + Redis
② Redirect: Client → Redirect Svc → Redis (cache hit) → 302
③ Cache miss: Redirect Svc → PostgreSQL → populate Redis → 302
④ Analytics: CDN logs → Kafka → Druid aggregation
⑤ Expiry: Cleanup Job scans PostgreSQL, purges Redis + CDN cache

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study consistent hashing and how it applies to distributing short codes across multiple Redis
shards — understanding virtual nodes and rebalancing is key for cache scaling.
Expert Note: Learn about Snowflake ID generation: the 41-bit timestamp, 10-bit machine ID, and 12-bit
sequence components, and how to handle clock skew across distributed nodes.
Expert Note: Understand CDN architecture: how edge PoPs cache content, how cache invalidation
propagates globally, and the trade-off between TTL-based expiry and explicit purge.
Expert Note: Study base62 encoding: why it's preferred over base64 for URLs (no +, /, = characters), and
how to map 64-bit integers to 7-8 character strings.
Expert Note: Learn about geo-IP databases (MaxMind GeoLite2): how they map IP ranges to countries,
their accuracy limitations, and how to integrate them into a streaming pipeline.

URL Shortener (Bitly)
